Art title 1: Station 1 Jesus in The Garden of Gethsemane
Artist Statement 1:
In this depiction of Jesus in Gethsemane I showed him on hands
and knees, with sweat drops of blood around him. I showed his
friends falling asleep on him. We see references to the garden of
Eden in this garden of Gethsemane, as we see the snake coming
near Jesus. Where the first man failed under temptation in Eden,
bringing their own death, Jesus would deny temptation, saying not
my will but yours, even to his own death. In the trees we also see
the cup from the last supper he had just shared with his friends. We
see references to other moments of feeling deserted by friends and
needing to rely on faith: Daniel and the lion's den, and Joseph
thrown in a pit by his brothers. Also look for hands of God spread
over him, like the wings of a hen protecting her young. We have
started this series with the main color being blue. We will move
more towards red, to depict the mounting pain and violence against him.
Story Title again: ANIMATION: Station 1 Jesus in The Garden of Gethsemane
excerpt:
(See video below) Then Jesus came with them to a place called Gethsemane, and he said to his disciples, "Sit here while I go over there and pray." He took along Peter and the two sons of Zebedee, and began to feel sorrow and distress. Then he said to them, "My soul is sorrowful even to death. Remain here and keep watch with me." He advanced a little and fell prostrate in prayer, saying, "My Father, if it is possible, let this cup pass from me; yet, not as I will, but as you will." When he returned to his disciples he found them asleep. He said to Peter, "So you could not keep watch with me for one hour? Watch and pray that you may not undergo the test. The spirit is willing, but the flesh is weak."
(Matthew 26:36--41, NAB)

Lord, grant us your strength and wisdom,
that we may seek to follow your will in all things.

Here we see Jesus in such emotional distress that he sweats drops of blood. Recognize the
sharp branches reaching toward him, and the snake whispering in his ear. Have you felt like
this? Let yourself feel it, write about it, and then sit in the understanding that Jesus intimately
knows this feeling as well.

Look at the disciples that could not stay awake with Jesus during his hard time. How did you feel when people haven't provided support during your hard times? Share a moment with him.
Remembering how hard it was, who is someone you could support right now, during their hard time?

Focus on the uplifted hands and the weapons around them. Consider the gravity Jesus felt approaching. He dreaded the suffering and isolation ahead. When, and in what ways, have you
felt this way? What would need to happen for you to be able to say, "Even so, your will, not my will, Lord."

Now we look into another garden, the garden of Eden, in which Adam and Eve were tempted.and, like us at times, have failed. The cup of Jesus's suffering and sacrifice has paid for your sins. As you color this image, consider its meaning and ask God what He wants to say to you.

We see a couple more images from the Old Testament that reinforce Jesus's situation. Although imminent danger stood before Daniel in the Lion's den, he chose to trust in his God. We see Joseph in the pit his brothers threw him into, left by those close to him. When have you felt like Daniel, vulnerable to danger, or like Joseph, abandoned and wronged by people you've trusted? Ask God to share His thoughts about your situations like this.

Not only did God restore these men from the Old Testament to greater power, but they were able to forgive those who caused them violence and, in fact, work for their prosperity. Ask God to bring to mind anyone you need to forgive. And forgive again. And forgive again.

Ask God to bring to mind anyone that you have wronged. Ask God, what is your role in their healing?

Let's turn our attention to God's hands hidden in the canopy of the trees, protecting everything beneath them. God the Father is taking care of His son Jesus, and the entire situation, during one of Jesus's hardest moments. Above the hands we also see the wings of a hen, like those of one protecting her young. Similarly God is watching over you even when things seem hard. Invite God to show you where He was present in a challenging time.
How do you see God working through it for what might become a greater outcome, a richer story?

Lastly, focus on the flag of surrender. God asks for your surrender so that He can give you something better in place of your will. Is He asking for you to surrender your anxiety? Your future? Your financial situation? Your anger? Or to share with Him your pain? What is He asking you to surrender? Let us use Jesus as our example of surrendering.

Amen.
3dNotes4: Museum of life in ancient Nazareth
